video_title: Volts ϟ 5.22.2026 – Kristin George Bagdanov + Panama Bartholomy (How To Phase Out Residential Gas)
gist: Building Decarbonization Coalition leaders argue the US residential gas system is in a self-reinforcing cost spiral — ~$49B/yr utility capex (two-thirds of the gas bill now infrastructure, not commodity) on a flat-to-declining demand base — and lay out the regulatory levers (line-extension allowances, fuel-neutral obligation-to-serve, future-of-gas proceedings, neighborhood-scale electrification and thermal energy networks) that will reroute that spend over the 2020s-2040s. Investor relevance: heat-pump demand is structurally strong (US now #1 globally at 63% market share), gas-distribution capex faces growing stranding/political risk, and shareholder-funded retirement-fund proposals could shift end-of-life liabilities from ratepayers to utility equity.

themes.theme: Gas death spiral & utility capex unwind

insights.claim: US gas utilities now spend ~$49B/year on their systems, ~$28B of it on distribution (pipes, services, meters); this capex has tripled since ~2010 while per-customer gas demand declines, meaning customers pay more for a product they use less.
insights.speaker: Kristin George Bagdanov (Building Decarbonization Coalition)
insights.so_what: Gas LDC distribution capex is on an unsustainable trajectory against falling throughput; investors in gas-only LDCs face rising regulatory scrutiny of capex prudence and eventual stranding risk.
insights.quote: We're at about $49 billion a year that gas utilities are spending on their systems, and about $28 billion of that is just on the distribution side system

insights.claim: Roughly two-thirds of the average US residential gas bill now goes to infrastructure rather than the gas commodity — the reverse of the 1980s ratio — because the regulated return model rewards capital spending regardless of utilization.
insights.speaker: Kristin George Bagdanov
insights.so_what: Bill composition has flipped to fixed/infrastructure costs, making gas bills rise even as usage falls — a setup for political backlash and rate-case losses for LDCs.
insights.quote: in the 80s and it's more like the reverse, where about two-thirds is going to the commodity cost and one-third is going to the infrastructure

insights.claim: Each $1 of gas-utility capex yields $2-$3 in customer costs over time because of shareholder returns and financing, so the rate-of-return model structurally incentivizes overbuilding ('gold plating') a shrinking system.
insights.speaker: Kristin George Bagdanov
insights.so_what: The 2-3x bill multiplier on capex is the engine of the spiral; reforms that cap or claw back this capex directly threaten LDC earnings growth.
insights.quote: Every dollar they spend in capital expenditures yields $2 to $3 in customer costs because of those shareholder returns and financing costs.

insights.claim: A defection rate as low as ~2% of gas customers can drive remaining-customer gas prices up ~50% over a decade, so the cost spiral does not require mass exit to become acute.
insights.speaker: host (David Roberts, citing a webinar model)
insights.so_what: The tipping point for gas-rate escalation is low — affluent-customer electrification in a few percent of homes can trigger steep rate increases and accelerate further defection.
insights.quote: about a 2% defection rate causes prices to rise by 50% over a decade

insights.claim: US gas bills rose ~60% faster than electric bills in the 2025 CPI, most of it infrastructure-driven, so the affordability crisis often framed as an electricity problem is already more acute on the gas side.
insights.speaker: Kristin George Bagdanov
insights.so_what: Gas affordability is a present, not future, political pressure point — supports regulatory momentum to curb gas capex.
insights.quote: we see that gas bills outpaced electric bills by 60%

insights.claim: The US has moved from #3 to the #1 heat-pump market globally in five years and now holds ~63% market share, with heat pumps outselling gas furnaces for four straight years and heat-pump water heaters accelerating.
insights.speaker: Panama Bartholomy (Building Decarbonization Coalition)
insights.so_what: Heat-pump demand is a rare US clean-energy growth leadership story (vs lagging EVs/batteries/renewables) — structurally bullish for heat-pump and HPWH manufacturers and installers.
insights.quote: we're talking 63% market share, where we can proudly stand up and say we were third place five years ago, we're now number one

insights.claim: Building decarbonization legislation held steady at the state level in 2025 despite expectations of a post-2024-election drop-off, with the same volume of bills on standards, incentives and education as 2024.
insights.speaker: Panama Bartholomy
insights.so_what: Policy tailwind for electrification appliances is more durable than the federal political narrative suggests — de-risks state-incentive-dependent demand.

insights.claim: Gas-appliance manufacturers themselves now universally describe the electrification transition as inevitable, signaling the supply chain at the end of the gas pipe is repositioning.
insights.speaker: Panama Bartholomy
insights.so_what: When incumbent gas-appliance OEMs concede inevitability, it confirms a durable product-mix shift toward electric/induction — a read-through for appliance-maker capex and product roadmaps.
insights.quote: When you talk to the manufacturers of gas appliances, universally, they say this transition is inevitable.

themes.theme: Regulatory levers: line-extension allowances & obligation to serve

insights.claim: Eliminating gas line-extension allowances (free new hookups subsidized by existing customers) nationally would save ratepayers an estimated $3-$7 billion/year, making it the highest-leverage near-term reform.
insights.speaker: Kristin George Bagdanov
insights.so_what: Killing hookup subsidies removes a large recurring transfer that props up gas-customer growth — a multi-billion-dollar headwind to LDC connection growth as more states adopt it.
insights.quote: The range was from $3 to $7 billion a year.

insights.claim: California removed electric line-extension allowances for mixed-fuel buildings, granting the subsidy only to all-electric developments — a two-sided incentive design that markedly shifted new builds toward electrification.
insights.speaker: Panama Bartholomy / Kristin George Bagdanov
insights.so_what: Pairing gas-disincentive with electric-only carrots is a proven lever to flip new-build economics — watch other states copy it; bearish for gas in new construction.
insights.quote: California has gone one step further where they have removed electric line extension allowances ... if it is to a mixed-fuel building

insights.claim: Six states plus DC are actively deliberating line-extension-allowance reform (incl. Maryland, Minnesota, Illinois) and ~8 states are pursuing utility-led thermal energy networks, indicating a broad multi-state regulatory wave rather than a California outlier.
insights.speaker: Kristin George Bagdanov
insights.so_what: The reform set is diffusing across states, raising the systemic regulatory risk to gas-distribution growth nationwide.

insights.claim: Many state 'obligation to serve' statutes do not mention gas and may already be interpretable as fuel-neutral (obligation to serve end-uses, all of which electricity can now meet), so the main barrier to swapping gas for electric is fear of litigation, not law.
insights.speaker: Kristin George Bagdanov
insights.so_what: Reinterpreting obligation-to-serve as fuel-neutral could legally unlock managed gas wind-downs; litigation risk and legislative clarification (e.g. Massachusetts dispute) are the gating factors to watch.
insights.quote: there's nothing legally stopping this other than the fear of litigation

insights.claim: New York scrapped its arbitrary 100-foot free-hookup rule but suspended implementation through end-2027 after industry opposition and a lawsuit, showing reforms can pass yet stall under legal and election-cycle pressure.
insights.speaker: host / Kristin George Bagdanov
insights.so_what: Implementation lag and litigation mean the gas-capex unwind will be uneven and reversible state-to-state — timing risk for theses that price in rapid policy change.

insights.claim: PG&E launched a Zonal Electrification Equity Pilot — the first public utility RFP for service-territory-wide neighborhood-scale electrification — with the first homes converting this month after winning neighborhood agreement.
insights.speaker: Panama Bartholomy
insights.so_what: Zonal electrification creates a new procurement channel (consultants, installers, analysis vendors) and a template for converting gas neighborhoods wholesale — watch PG&E pilot outcomes as a scaling proof point.
insights.quote: Pacific Gas and Electric has the Zonal Electrification Equity Pilot Program. It's the first public RFP that's been released by a utility ... neighborhood-scale electrification.

insights.claim: California reduced the neighborhood-conversion approval threshold from 100% to two-thirds of building owners, removing the single-holdout veto that blocks zonal gas decommissioning.
insights.speaker: Panama Bartholomy
insights.so_what: Lowering the consent threshold materially improves the feasibility/economics of decommissioning whole gas segments — a structural enabler for at-scale conversion.
insights.quote: reduced the threshold for approval for a project like this from 100% of building owners down to only two-thirds

insights.claim: Early zonal-electrification outreach offers homeowners ~$35,000 checks to electrify in lieu of a coming pipeline replacement, framing whole-neighborhood gas shutoff as cheaper than reinvesting in the pipe.
insights.speaker: Panama Bartholomy
insights.so_what: Avoided-pipeline-replacement economics can fund large per-home electrification incentives — a demand pull for heat-pump/appliance installers funded by deferred gas capex.
insights.quote: instead give you a $35,000 check to make upgrades to your house

insights.claim: California identified 150+ priority zones for neighborhood-scale pilots and New York has ~10 utility-led thermal energy network pilots nearing construction greenlight, with one-in-four states holding TEN-related legislation.
insights.speaker: Kristin George Bagdanov
entities: Utility Thermal Energy Network and Jobs Act
insights.so_what: A visible pipeline of pilots gives geothermal/thermal-network developers and engineering firms a near-term addressable market; ~few-hundred successful projects over five years is the stated scaling threshold.

insights.claim: Gas-only utilities may become advocates for thermal energy networks because TENs preserve a pipe-based business and workforce, whereas all-electric conversion eliminates them entirely.
insights.speaker: host / Panama Bartholomy
insights.so_what: Survival incentives could turn gas LDCs from blockers into champions of thermal networks — a possible pivot path (gas-to-thermal utility) for otherwise-stranded gas-only operators.

insights.claim: Utilities are installing pipes designed to last decades — described as a seven-year investment in a system meant to be retired in ~20 years — in states with earlier decarbonization deadlines, building assets destined to be stranded or to blow through carbon targets.
insights.speaker: Panama Bartholomy
insights.so_what: Ongoing gas-pipe replacement in decarbonizing states is accumulating stranding risk now; the question of who absorbs the write-off (ratepayers vs shareholders) is becoming a live policy fight.
insights.quote: we're also gold-plating the gas system in a seven-year investment in a system we want to be off of in 20 years

insights.claim: California's Senator Stern introduced legislation for a shareholder-funded utility retirement fund, aiming to shift gas-system end-of-life costs from ratepayers onto utility shareholders.
insights.speaker: Panama Bartholomy
insights.so_what: If shareholder-funded decommissioning becomes a model, it materially raises end-of-life liability for gas-LDC equity holders — a direct valuation risk to watch in IOU exposure.
insights.quote: a retirement fund paid for by shareholders of the utilities in order to force this conversation forward

insights.claim: Massachusetts now requires utilities to evaluate non-pipeline alternatives (NPAs) before approving new gas pipeline projects, removing the default to status-quo expansion.
insights.speaker: Kristin George Bagdanov
insights.so_what: Mandatory NPA evaluation inserts a cost-effectiveness gate that can systematically divert capex away from gas pipes toward electrification/thermal alternatives.

insights.claim: 14 states containing ~47% of US residential gas customers have opened future-of-gas proceedings, making the managed-transition question a near-majority-of-market regulatory reality rather than a fringe debate.
insights.speaker: Panama Bartholomy
insights.so_what: Nearly half the residential gas market is under active future-of-gas review — broad regulatory exposure for gas-LDC business models across the country.
insights.quote: Those 14 states — you have just about 47%. Just under half of all residential gas customers in the country are in those 14 states.
